reasoning_summary: For run 20260421_105134_bf16_gemm_v1_8dcab81 at 24.186960 ms, the richer NCU handoff changes the live-queue ranking materially. The dominant evidence is 200 registers per thread, only 16.63% active warps, and a register-limited 2-block occupancy ceiling; barrier cost is real at 5.47% but is secondary to the live-state problem. That demotes export-address and grouped-row replay families, keeps the exact restore only as a recovery guardrail, and promotes the families that can either lower live state directly or cut steady-state wait/sync overhead without reopening the 22 KB shared-memory blowup.

family_audit.reason: This is the strongest live family against the new primary hotspot because it is the only active branch whose historical rationale explicitly moved register pressure and active-warps off the 16.6% plateau.
The accepted PTX surface is still the cleanest place to reduce live fragments and control-path lifetime while preserving the fastest correctness-proven dispatch.
Barrier is secondary but still actionable, and this family is the best way to attack the wait/sync seam without repeating the x32 shared-memory footprint mistake.
Keep the exact restore live as the lowest-risk recovery anchor, but the richer NCU summary says it does not attack the current register/occupancy hotspot directly.
The family still fits the barrier evidence, but it ranks below the top barrier-trim family because it is narrower and more correctness-fragile.
This remains a valid secondary barrier family, but the richer NCU evidence now says barrier is not the only or dominant ceiling.
A plain 256x128 pivot still addresses the occupancy class, but the richer NCU evidence favors the lower-register transplant variant over the unmodified pivot.
This historical family still matters because it once moved live-state pressure strongly, but the safer correctness-preserving transplant path should be tried first.
The K-loop schedule family stays relevant once a 256x128 branch is active, but it is a follow-on move rather than the first register-pressure probe.
The richer NCU handoff does not show a dominant export-side hotspot; registers, occupancy, and wait/sync now look more important than epilogue integer cleanup.
Minimal export cleanup is now a tertiary fallback because the structured NCU summary no longer supports export arithmetic as the dominant bottleneck class.
Grouped-row replay is still a plausible alternate surface, but the new NCU evidence points to live-state pressure inside the current PTX path before another traversal replay.
The non-PTX sibling remains a useful alternate surface, but it does not address the current PTX register hotspot as directly as the promoted families.
Traversal changes are now below the cut line because the richer NCU evidence is dominated by occupancy and live-state pressure rather than inter-CTA ordering.
This remains only an A/B guardrail candidate; it does not provide a new bottleneck attack under the richer NCU reading.

directions.name: Transplant low-register half-panel staging into the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot
directions.hypothesis: The richer NCU summary says the live queue should prioritize the only active family that directly attacks 200 registers per thread and the 16.63% active-warps ceiling. The correctness-safe 256x128 pivot should keep its current writer ownership, but transplant the compact staging and register-budget ideas that historically moved occupancy in the right direction.
directions.expected_bottleneck: occupancy_latency_hiding_issue with tensor_core_underutilization driven by register pressure and oversized live state
directions.risk: High: this is the best hotspot-aligned family, but it touches a broader geometry and could reintroduce correctness or shared-budget regressions if the old ownership split leaks back in.
ranking_notes: This family moved to rank 1 because the rich NCU handoff makes registers/thread the dominant hotspot instead of export bookkeeping.
It is ahead of the PTX-local retune because it is the only active family with explicit historical evidence of breaking the low-warp plateau.
The risk is real, but the queue needs at least one forward candidate that attacks the primary bottleneck directly.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers per thread stay at or above 200, if active warps do not improve, or if writer ownership changes trigger correctness failures.

directions.name: Trim live state inside the active 128x128 PTX control path before more epilogue work
directions.hypothesis: The richer NCU summary weakens the export-address thesis and strengthens a different PTX-local read: the accepted 128x128 PTX microkernel is carrying too much live state through its steady-state control path. Retuning fragment lifetime, grouped-row control state, or export handoff sequencing on the accepted surface should be tried before another epilogue-only cleanup.

directions.risk: Medium: this stays on the fastest correctness-proven branch, but lifetime and handoff changes can still flatten gains if they only shuffle stalls around without reducing registers.
ranking_notes: This became the best PTX-local family after the richer NCU summary showed that registers and occupancy matter more than export address arithmetic.
It stays below dir_01 because the 256x128 transplant attacks the primary hotspot more directly.
It stays above the barrier-only families because a register drop on the accepted surface can improve both latency hiding and barrier sensitivity at once.
